status_legend.in_progress: The feature is the current active task.
status_legend.blocked: Work cannot continue until a documented blocker is resolved.
status_legend.passing: Required verification has passed and evidence is recorded.

features.name: Document ingest & chunking
features.description: Parse uploaded PDF/PPTX/DOCX files and split into chunks by heading/slide boundary (not a hard character count), producing chunks[] with doc_id, page, and chunk_id. See docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md step 1.
features.user_visible_behavior: POST /ingest on packages/gpu-worker accepts multipart file uploads (PDF/PPTX/DOCX) and returns {"chunks": [...]} with doc_id/chunk_id/page/text populated per chunk. /embed is left for feat-002 (embedding + concept extraction).
verification: Run packages/gpu-worker with a sample PDF/PPTX/DOCX from data/ and confirm chunks[] is returned with doc_id, page, chunk_id populated for each chunk.
Confirm no chunk splits a heading/slide boundary in the middle for the sample set.
evidence: packages/gpu-worker/worker/ingest.py implements chunk_document() (PDF: one chunk per page via PyMuPDF; PPTX: one chunk per slide via python-pptx; DOCX: one chunk per Heading-delimited section via python-docx).

packages/gpu-worker/tests/test_ingest.py — 4/4 passed (`python -m pytest tests/`): PDF splits by page, PPTX splits by slide, DOCX splits on heading boundaries without cutting a section in half, unsupported extensions raise ValueError.

Manually exercised POST /ingest via FastAPI TestClient with a synthetic PDF: 200 response, {'chunks': [{'doc_id': 'sample.pdf', 'chunk_id': 'sample.pdf:p1', 'page': 1, 'text': 'Test ingest endpoint page one.'}]}.

No real sample PDF/PPTX/DOCX exists in data/ yet (only the mastery-tree sample JSON) — verified against synthetic fixtures built in the test file instead; re-verify against the actual demo dataset once feat-009 picks a document set.
features.notes: worker/main.py's /embed now implements feat-002. docx has no rendered page numbers, so its `page` field is the 1-indexed heading-delimited section instead — documented in ingest.py.

features.name: Embedding + concept extraction & clustering
features.description: Vectorize chunks with sentence-transformers (bge-large/e5-large) on ROCm-enabled PyTorch, extract raw_concepts[] per chunk via Gemma (2B/7B) served locally through vLLM-ROCm/Ollama, then cluster/dedupe into canonical_concepts[] via cosine similarity + Louvain clustering (no LLM call needed for dedupe). See docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md steps 2-3.
features.user_visible_behavior: packages/gpu-worker/worker/main.py's /embed endpoint returns canonical_concepts[] (name, short definition, originating chunk_id) instead of the current stub.
verification: Run the sample demo dataset through the endpoint and confirm canonical_concepts[] merges near-duplicate phrasings (e.g. 'gradient descent' vs 'steepest-descent optimization') into one node.
Confirm Gemma is invoked via a local ROCm/Ollama endpoint, not a hosted API call — this is required for the Best Use of Gemma bonus and AMD platform usage criterion (see docs/brd.md §4.3, §7.3).
evidence: packages/gpu-worker/worker/concepts.py implements GemmaConceptExtractor (HTTP client against {GEMMA_BASE_URL}/api/generate — an Ollama-style local endpoint, default http://localhost:11434), SentenceTransformerEmbedder (lazy-loaded bge-large-en-v1.5), and cluster_concepts (cosine similarity + networkx.algorithms.community.louvain_communities, no LLM call).

packages/gpu-worker/tests/test_concepts.py — 4/4 passed: confirms GemmaConceptExtractor POSTs to '{base_url}/api/generate' (asserted literally, so a regression to a hosted-API URL would fail this test); confirms cluster_concepts merges 'gradient descent' and 'the steepest-descent optimization algorithm' into one canonical concept (shorter name wins) while keeping 'linear regression' separate; confirms extract_raw_concepts aggregates across chunks; confirms empty input returns [].

packages/gpu-worker/tests/test_main_embed.py — 1/1 passed: exercises the real POST /embed endpoint end-to-end via FastAPI TestClient with fakes swapped in through app.dependency_overrides (get_gemma_extractor, get_embedder), proving the endpoint wiring itself (not just the underlying functions) works.
Full suite: `python -m pytest tests/` — 9/9 passed (4 feat-001 + 5 feat-002, no regressions). `python -m compileall worker tests` — pass.

Not run against a live local Gemma/Ollama server or a real sentence-transformers model download — both are behind fakes in the test suite by design (network/GPU-independent tests). Re-verify against a real vLLM-ROCm/Ollama instance on the AMD Developer Cloud box once that's provisioned (see docs/hackathon-scope.md §5 Day-1 ROCm check).
features.notes: GEMMA_BASE_URL env var controls the local endpoint (default http://localhost:11434, matching Ollama's default port); no hosted-API fallback is wired in on purpose. Update: CanonicalConcept now also carries `id` (concept_NNN) and `embedding` (cluster centroid), returned from /embed — added while implementing feat-003, which needs both for pair pre-filtering without re-embedding concepts from scratch.

features.name: Dependency-graph construction (prerequisite inference)
features.description: For pre-filtered concept pairs only (high embedding similarity or nearby co-occurrence), call Fireworks AI (Llama 3.x) to infer directed prerequisite edges with a confidence score. See docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md step 4 and the O(n^2) cost-avoidance note.
features.user_visible_behavior: packages/gpu-worker/worker/main.py's POST /build-graph endpoint takes /embed's concepts[] output and returns {"edges": [...]} (candidate_edges[] as from/to/confidence, keyed by concept id) instead of the current NotImplementedError stub.
verification: Confirm the number of Fireworks calls scales with pre-filtered pairs, not all-pairs O(n^2), for the demo dataset.
Spot-check a few candidate_edges[] against the sample tree in docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md for plausibility.
evidence: packages/gpu-worker/worker/prerequisites.py implements pre_filter_pairs (cosine similarity over concept embeddings from feat-002's /embed output) and FireworksClient (hosted OpenAI-compatible chat completions — a hosted call is fine here, unlike Gemma extraction, since this isn't the AMD-usage/Gemma-bonus story) and build_candidate_edges.

packages/gpu-worker/tests/test_prerequisites.py — 4/4 passed. test_pre_filter_pairs_avoids_full_all_pairs_scan directly proves the O(n^2) avoidance: 6 concepts in 3 tight similarity clusters yield C(6,2)=15 possible pairs, but pre_filter_pairs returns exactly the 3 within-cluster pairs. test_build_candidate_edges_only_calls_fireworks_for_prefiltered_pairs asserts the fake Fireworks client is called exactly 3 times (once per pre-filtered pair), not 15.

packages/gpu-worker/tests/test_main_build_graph.py — 1/1 passed: exercises the real POST /build-graph endpoint via FastAPI TestClient + app.dependency_overrides (no real Fireworks API key/network needed).
Full suite: `python -m pytest tests/` — 14/14 passed (4 feat-001 + 5 feat-002 + 5 feat-003, no regressions). `python -m compileall worker tests` — pass.

Not yet spot-checked against the actual sample tree in docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md — that requires a real Fireworks API key and a real concept set from feat-002 run end-to-end; do this once feat-009 picks the demo dataset.
Not run against a live Fireworks API — FireworksClient is only verified against a monkeypatched httpx.post.
features.notes: Corrected this feature's `area` from "api" to "gpu-worker" — docs/architecture.md's Data contract section says gpu-worker produces the candidate graph (including inferred prerequisite edges) and hands it to api, which starts at the self-checking validation loop (feat-004). The original area value was inconsistent with that. CanonicalConcept (feat-002) gained `id` and `embedding` fields to support this feature's pre-filtering — see concepts.py.

features.name: Self-checking graph agent (cycle/redundancy repair)
features.description: Build a networkx.DiGraph from candidate_edges[], run nx.simple_cycles(), and repair any cycle found by dropping its lowest-confidence edge (bounded retry loop; force-drop the weakest edge if a cycle survives N iterations). See docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md step 5.
features.user_visible_behavior: The API produces a valid_dag with zero cycles for the demo dataset, with a log of any edges the agent auto-dropped and why.
verification: Feed a deliberately cyclic edge set (e.g. an inverted prerequisite) through the repair loop and confirm it converges to a valid DAG within N iterations.
Confirm nx.simple_cycles() returns empty on the final graph.
evidence: apps/api/app/services/graph.py implements build_graph, repair_cycles (bounded loop dropping the lowest-confidence edge per detected cycle, then a force-drop fallback while-loop if a cycle survives max_iterations), and validate_graph. Wired via a new POST /graph/validate endpoint (apps/api/app/routers/graph.py), mounted in app/main.py.

apps/api/tests/test_graph.py — 5/5 passed: acyclic input passes through unchanged; a 3-node cycle with one inverted-prerequisite edge (a->b->c->a, weakest edge c->a at 0.3) is repaired by dropping exactly that edge, confirmed via nx.simple_cycles() returning empty on the result; two independent 2-cycles with max_iterations=1 (fewer than the 2 drops needed) prove the force-drop fallback actually fires — first drop logged with an 'iteration N' reason, second with the 'force-dropped' reason, final graph still acyclic; validate_graph's combined edges+dropped_edges output shape.
apps/api/tests/test_graph_endpoint.py — 1/1 passed: exercises the real POST /graph/validate endpoint via FastAPI TestClient (no fakes needed — step 5 is pure networkx logic, no external API calls).
This is apps/api's first test suite — added apps/api/requirements-dev.txt (pytest) and wired `cd apps/api && python -m pytest tests/` into init.sh/CLAUDE.md.
Full run: `python -m compileall app tests` (pass); `python -m pytest tests/` — 6/6 passed in apps/api.
features.notes: This is the 'agentic self-check' the hackathon differentiation story leans on (docs/hackathon-scope.md §3) — the repair-loop logging (dropped_edges[], each with a from/to/confidence/reason) is what makes it auditable rather than a silent filter; don't drop that when this gets consumed downstream. nx.simple_cycles() is exponential in the worst case — fine for the hackathon's sparse, pre-filtered graphs (dozens/hundreds of nodes), but worth watching if the demo dataset ends up much larger or denser.

features.name: Topological sort → tiered learning path
features.description: Use nx.topological_generations() (not a plain topological_sort) on the valid_dag to assign each node a level, allowing parallel branches. See docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md step 6.
features.user_visible_behavior: Each node in the final graph JSON has a level field; apps/api/app/routers/trees.py's GET /trees/{topic_id} returns nodes[] + edges[] matching the schema in docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md instead of the current 501 stub.
verification: Confirm every edge's child level is strictly greater than its parent's level (DAG tier invariant) for the demo dataset.
Confirm GET /trees/{topic_id} returns real data, not the current HTTPException(501).
evidence: apps/api/app/services/levels.py implements assign_levels(edges, concepts) — builds a DiGraph seeded with every concept id (not just ones that appear in an edge, so isolated concepts still get a tier-0 node), then groups via nx.topological_generations().

apps/api/tests/test_levels.py — 4/4 passed: a two-branch-converging graph (mirrors the sample tree in docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md) confirms tiers c1=0, c2=0, c3=1, c4=1, c5=2, and directly asserts the DAG tier invariant (child level > parent level) for every edge; isolated no-edge concepts get tier 0 + unlocked status; non-zero tiers are marked locked; chunk_ids carry through into a sources[] list.

Wired GET /trees/{topic_id} in apps/api/app/routers/trees.py to serve data/atlas_mastery_tree_sample.json (path resolved from the repo root, overridable via SAMPLE_TREE_PATH) instead of the 501 stub — this was the file's own pre-existing TODO comment's recommendation, not a new design decision.

apps/api/tests/test_trees_endpoint.py — 1/1 passed: GET /trees/intro-to-ml returns 200 with non-empty nodes[]/edges[], and independently re-checks the DAG tier invariant against the real checked-in sample dataset (not just synthetic test data).
Full run: apps/api `python -m pytest tests/` — 11/11 passed (5 feat-004 + 6 feat-005, no regressions). packages/gpu-worker re-run — 14/14, no regressions. `python -m compileall app tests` — pass.
features.notes: GET /trees/{topic_id} is NOT yet wired to the live pipeline (feat-001..004's actual output) — it serves the static sample file regardless of topic_id, exactly as the original TODO comment described ("For now, load the sample JSON in /data for local dev"). assign_levels() itself is fully implemented and tested as a pure function; composing it with a live feat-002/feat-004 run into this endpoint is a bigger integration task, likely alongside feat-009 (pre-indexed demo dataset) rather than this feature. Also: data/ is not copied into the Docker image (docker/api.Dockerfile only COPYs apps/api/) — SAMPLE_TREE_PATH env var is the escape hatch for that once containerized deployment needs it.

features.name: Skill-tree UI with unlock logic
features.description: Render nodes[]/edges[] with @xyflow/react, grouped into columns by level and colored by status (locked | unlocked | mastered). A node unlocks when every edge pointing into it is mastered.
features.user_visible_behavior: apps/web/app/tree/page.tsx shows an interactive skill tree fetched from data/atlas_mastery_tree_sample.json (dev) or GET {NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL}/trees/{topic_id}, replacing the current 'TODO: render mastery tree' placeholder.
verification: Load the sample tree JSON in the browser and confirm nodes render grouped by tier with correct lock/unlock coloring.
Manually mark a Tier-0 node mastered and confirm its children flip from locked to unlocked without a page reload.
evidence: apps/web/app/tree/{types,unlock,positions,progressStore,TreeNode,page}.{ts,tsx} implement the tree view: unlock.ts derives each node's locked/unlocked/completed status from a single source of truth (a completed-id set) plus edge structure, rather than trusting a possibly-stale status field; positions.ts lays out nodes into columns by level (not trusting a position field, since apps/api's assign_levels() output doesn't have one — only the static sample file does); progressStore.ts (zustand) holds completedIds client-side so completing a node re-renders unlocked children without a page reload; page.tsx fetches GET {NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL}/trees/{topic_id} and renders via @xyflow/react.

Fixed a real bug during implementation: a file named services/positions.ts was originally named layout.ts, which collides with Next.js App Router's reserved `layout.ts` filename convention inside app/ and broke the build with a cryptic type error. Renamed to positions.ts.
`npm run lint` — no warnings/errors. `npm run build` — compiles and type-checks (this caught the layout.ts collision).

Manually verified live in a browser (not just typecheck/build): started apps/api (uvicorn) and apps/web (next dev), used Playwright (installed temporarily for this check) to load /tree and screenshot it. Confirmed: tier-0 nodes 'Linear Algebra Basics'/'Probability Basics' render green (completed, from the sample data's seed status), 'Loss Functions' renders blue (unlocked, both its prerequisites are already completed), 'Gradient Descent'/'Neural Networks' render grey (locked). Clicked 'Loss Functions' and confirmed it turned green and 'Gradient Descent' flipped grey->blue immediately, with 'Neural Networks' correctly still locked — no page reload, no browser console errors.
Full run: apps/api 11/11, packages/gpu-worker 14/14 — both re-checked, no regressions from this frontend-only change.
features.notes: Reconciled the status-naming mismatch flagged in feat-005's handoff: tailwind.config.ts already defines `locked`/`unlocked`/`completed` colors (not `mastered`), matching the sample dataset — so the UI (and unlock.ts) uses "completed", and docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md's schema wording ("mastered") is the stale one, worth fixing there for consistency. The click-to-complete interaction is a demo-only stand-in for feat-008's real checkpoint-quiz submission — it currently "passes" any unlocked node on a single click with no quiz UI at all.

features.name: Per-node lesson + quiz + real-world example (agentic RAG)
features.description: For a given node, retrieve only that node's source chunks (via chunk_id/doc_id carried through since step 1), then call Fireworks to generate a lesson, an MCQ checkpoint quiz, and a real-world example, with a self-check that the quiz matches the lesson before returning it.
features.user_visible_behavior: An endpoint returns { lesson, quiz, example, sources } for a node_id, scoped to that node's cited chunks only — not whole-corpus prompting.
verification: Confirm the retrieval set passed to Fireworks is limited to the node's own sources[], not the full corpus.
Confirm the self-check step rejects/regenerates a quiz that doesn't match its lesson at least once during testing (force a mismatch to verify the guard fires).
evidence: apps/api/app/services/teach.py implements FireworksClient (generate() for lesson/quiz/example, check_alignment() for the self-check) and generate_lesson_package() — a bounded retry loop (default max_attempts=2) that regenerates the whole lesson/quiz/example package when check_alignment() reports a mismatch, and always returns a result (flagged via self_check.passed=False rather than raising) if every attempt still fails, so a demo never hard-crashes on a stubborn misalignment.

apps/api/app/routers/teach.py wires POST /trees/{topic_id}/nodes/{node_id}/lesson via FastAPI Depends(get_fireworks_client) (same dependency-override pattern as gpu-worker's /embed and /build-graph). The endpoint only ever sees the chunks the caller puts in the request body — there is no corpus-wide chunk store wired in yet (see notes below), so scoping is enforced by the request shape itself, not by a filter that could regress.

apps/api/tests/test_teach.py — 4/4 passed: first-attempt pass-through; test_generate_lesson_package_retrieval_is_scoped_to_only_this_nodes_chunks asserts the exact chunk list sent to FireworksClient.generate() equals the input chunks (nothing added); test_generate_lesson_package_regenerates_on_a_forced_quiz_lesson_mismatch forces check_alignment() to fail on attempt 1 and pass on attempt 2, and asserts generate() was actually called twice (a real regeneration, not just a re-check) and the returned lesson is the v2 (regenerated) one; test_generate_lesson_package_flags_unpassed_self_check_after_exhausting_attempts forces both attempts to fail and confirms the function still returns the last attempt with self_check.passed=False instead of raising.

apps/api/tests/test_teach_endpoint.py — 1/1 passed: exercises the real POST endpoint via FastAPI TestClient + app.dependency_overrides (no real Fireworks API key/network needed), confirming sources[] in the response strips chunk text down to chunk_id/doc_id/page only (citations, not the raw excerpt).

Full run: `python -m compileall app tests` (pass); `python -m pytest tests/` in apps/api — 16/16 passed (5 feat-004 + 6 feat-005 + 5 feat-007, no regressions). packages/gpu-worker re-run — 14/14, no regressions. `./init.sh` (web lint/build + both python suites) — all green.
features.notes: Scope decision: apps/api has no persisted chunk-text store yet (docs/hackathon-scope.md §5's SQLite/JSON storage line item hasn't been built as its own feature) — chunks only ever existed ephemerally inside a single /ingest→/embed request in gpu-worker. Rather than inventing a chunk store as a side effect of this feature, POST /trees/{topic_id}/nodes/{node_id}/lesson takes the node's own chunks[] (chunk_id/doc_id/page/text) directly in the request body; the caller (eventually feat-009's pre-indexing run, or a future storage layer) is responsible for looking those up by the node's sources[]. This mirrors feat-005's precedent of explicitly punting live-pipeline wiring to a later integration pass. Can proceed in parallel with feat-006 once feat-005 is done — both only need the tiered graph, not the finished UI.

features.name: Checkpoint quiz & unlock mechanic
features.description: Wire the MCQ quiz UI to POST /trees/{topic_id}/nodes/{node_id}/submit-quiz; on pass, mark the node mastered and unlock its children per feat-006's unlock logic.
features.user_visible_behavior: Passing a node's quiz in the UI immediately unlocks its downstream nodes, replacing the current 501 stub in apps/api/app/routers/trees.py's submit_quiz.
verification: Submit a passing quiz answer set and confirm the node's status flips to mastered and children flip to unlocked in the UI.
Submit a failing answer set and confirm the node stays locked/unlocked (not mastered) and children stay locked.
evidence: apps/api/app/services/quiz.py implements grade_quiz(quiz, answers) — a pure function scoring a submitted answer set against a node's questions[] (id/answer_index) and pass_threshold, returning {score, correct, total, passed}; a missing answer for a question counts as wrong rather than raising.

apps/api/app/routers/trees.py's POST /trees/{topic_id}/nodes/{node_id}/submit-quiz replaces the 501 stub: loads the tree (shared _load_tree() helper, refactored out of GET /trees/{topic_id}), looks up the node's quiz by node_id (404 if the node doesn't exist), and grades it via grade_quiz(). Does not persist status server-side — apps/api still has no storage layer (same scope note as feat-007) — so the response is graded fresh each call; apps/web's progressStore (feat-006) remains the source of truth for which nodes are completed, and only calls markCompleted() when this endpoint reports passed=true.

apps/api/tests/test_quiz.py — 4/4 passed: all-correct passes; a below-threshold set fails; an unanswered question counts as wrong (not an error); a single-question quiz meeting the threshold exactly still passes (boundary case).

apps/api/tests/test_trees_submit_quiz_endpoint.py — 3/3 passed against the real checked-in sample dataset (node n3 'Loss Functions', question q_n3_1, answer_index 1, pass_threshold 0.7): correct answer -> passed=true/score=1.0; wrong answer -> passed=false/score=0.0; unknown node_id -> 404.

apps/web: added app/tree/QuizModal.tsx (renders the node's quiz.questions[] as radio-button MCQs, disables Submit until every question is answered, shows a pass/fail message inline). Extended types.ts (Quiz/QuizQuestion, RawTreeNode.quiz, DisplayNode.quiz) and unlock.ts (carries quiz through toDisplayNodes()) so the quiz definition reaches the click handler. page.tsx's handleNodeClick now opens the modal for an unlocked node with a quiz (instead of feat-006's demo-only 'any click passes it' stand-in); submitQuiz() POSTs to the new endpoint and calls markCompleted() only when passed is true, reusing feat-006's existing progressStore/unlock.ts unchanged.
`npm run lint` (clean) and `npm run build` (compiles + type-checks) after the frontend changes.

Manually verified live in a browser (not just typecheck/build): started apps/api (uvicorn) and apps/web (next dev — port 3001, 3000 was already taken), used Playwright (installed temporarily, then removed) to drive /tree. Found and fixed a real bug in the process: an orphaned uvicorn process from an earlier session was still bound to port 8000 serving the pre-feat-008 code, so the first submit-quiz request 501'd even though the source was already correct — killed the stale process, started a fresh one, and confirmed the fix via curl before re-testing in the browser. Clicked the unlocked 'Loss Functions' node to open its quiz modal; submitted the wrong option first and confirmed 'Not quite — 0/1 correct. Try again.' with the modal staying open and the tree unchanged; then submitted the correct option and confirmed 'Passed! (1/1 correct)'. Closed the modal and confirmed (screenshot-verified) 'Loss Functions' turned green (completed) and 'Gradient Descent' flipped from grey/locked to blue/unlocked immediately, with 'Neural Networks' correctly still locked — no page reload, no console errors.
Full run: apps/api 23/23 (5 feat-004 + 6 feat-005 + 5 feat-007 + 4 feat-008-quiz + 3 feat-008-endpoint, no regressions), packages/gpu-worker 14/14. `./init.sh` end-to-end — all green.
features.notes: Grading is stateless/re-derived per request (no DB), consistent with feat-006's design of trusting the graph structure + a client-side completed-id set over any server-persisted status field. A real storage layer (to persist completed status server-side, e.g. for multi-device/session continuity) is still open — same gap flagged in feat-007's notes, worth addressing together in a future storage-layer feature rather than per-endpoint.

features.name: Pre-indexed, deterministic demo dataset
features.description: Run the full feat-001..feat-007 pipeline once, offline, on the chosen demo document set (10-30 docs, one domain) and save the static output so the live demo doesn't depend on Fireworks latency/availability during judging.
features.user_visible_behavior: data/atlas_mastery_tree_sample.json (or a topic-specific equivalent) is real pipeline output, not a hand-written placeholder, and the demo path loads it without calling Fireworks live.
verification: Run the demo end-to-end offline (no network calls to Fireworks) using only the pre-indexed JSON and confirm the full upload→graph→play loop still works.
Confirm the checked-in JSON matches the schema in docs/concept-graph-pipeline.md.
features.notes: This is the credit-runway and live-demo-reliability safeguard called out in docs/brd.md §II and docs/hackathon-scope.md §6 (Day 5).
